name: dompurify-expert
description: DOMPurify HTML sanitization expert for preventing XSS attacks in React applications, covering sanitization strategies, configuration options, React integration, centralized security patterns, and SOC2 compliance

can_do: Sanitize HTML to prevent XSS attacks
Handle user comments and forum posts
cannot_do: Decide business rules for what content is allowed (opinion)
Choose between sanitizing at storage vs render time (opinion)

dompurify_definition.statement: DOMPurify is a DOM-only, super-fast, uber-tolerant XSS sanitizer for HTML, MathML, and SVG. It sanitizes HTML by parsing, filtering, and reconstructing it to remove malicious content while preserving safe elements. De facto standard for XSS prevention in web applications

xss_prevention.statement: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) occurs when untrusted data is rendered as HTML without sanitization. Attackers inject <script> tags, event handlers (onerror, onclick), or javascript: URLs to execute malicious code. DOMPurify prevents XSS by removing all potentially dangerous elements and attributes

npm_install.statement: Install DOMPurify with: npm install dompurify. For TypeScript: npm install --save-dev @types/dompurify. DOMPurify is client-side only (requires DOM). For SSR use isomorphic-dompurify

sanitize_method.statement: DOMPurify.sanitize(html, config?) sanitizes HTML string and returns clean string. Takes dirty HTML as input, optional config object. Returns sanitized HTML safe to render. Example: const clean = DOMPurify.sanitize(dirtyHtml);
sanitize_method.example: import DOMPurify from 'dompurify'; const clean = DOMPurify.sanitize(''); // Returns:

react_integration.statement: In React, use DOMPurify with dangerouslySetInnerHTML: <div dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: DOMPurify.sanitize(html) }} />. NEVER use dangerouslySetInnerHTML without sanitization for user content

allowed_tags.statement: ALLOWED_TAGS config option whitelists HTML tags. Example: { ALLOWED_TAGS: ['p', 'br', 'strong', 'em', 'a'] }. Only specified tags are kept, all others removed. Whitelist approach is safest

allowed_attr.statement: ALLOWED_ATTR config option whitelists attributes. Example: { ALLOWED_ATTR: ['href', 'title'] }. Only specified attributes kept on elements. Critical for preventing event handler attacks (onclick, onerror)

forbid_tags.statement: FORBID_TAGS config option blacklists specific tags. Example: { FORBID_TAGS: ['script', 'style', 'iframe'] }. Specified tags always removed. Use with ALLOWED_TAGS for defense in depth

allow_data_attr.statement: ALLOW_DATA_ATTR config option controls data-* attributes. Default: true. Set to false for stricter sanitization. Example: { ALLOW_DATA_ATTR: false } removes all data-* attributes

return_dom.statement: RETURN_DOM and RETURN_DOM_FRAGMENT options return DOM node or DocumentFragment instead of string. Useful for programmatic DOM manipulation. Example: { RETURN_DOM: true }

strict.statement: Strict sanitization for user comments, forum posts, untrusted input. Allow only: bold, italic, emphasis, strong, links, line breaks. Forbid: images, custom classes, data attributes. Example config: { ALLOWED_TAGS: ['b', 'i', 'em', 'strong', 'a', 'br'], ALLOWED_ATTR: ['href'], ALLOW_DATA_ATTR: false }

moderate.statement: Moderate sanitization for rich text editors, internal content. Allow: paragraphs, headings, lists, formatting, links, code blocks. Forbid: scripts, iframes, objects. Example config: { ALLOWED_TAGS: ['p', 'h1', 'h2', 'h3', 'ul', 'ol', 'li', 'strong', 'em', 'code', 'pre', 'a'], ALLOWED_ATTR: ['href', 'title', 'class'] }

lenient.statement: Lenient sanitization for trusted internal sources, admin content. Allow: full formatting, images, tables, divs. Still forbid: scripts, iframes, dangerous event handlers. Example config: { ALLOWED_TAGS: ['p', 'div', 'span', 'h1-h6', 'ul', 'ol', 'li', 'img', 'table', 'tr', 'td'], ALLOW_DATA_ATTR: true }

rich_text_editors.statement: Rich text editor output (Quill, TinyMCE, Draft.js) must be sanitized before rendering. Editors may allow HTML that can be exploited. Use moderate sanitization: allow formatting tags, forbid dangerous tags

user_comments.statement: User comments require strict sanitization. Allow only basic formatting (bold, italic, links). Forbid: scripts, images, custom styling. Prevents user-to-user XSS attacks

markdown_html.statement: Markdown converted to HTML (via marked, showdown, etc.) must be sanitized. Markdown parsers may generate HTML with XSS vectors. Sanitize after conversion with moderate config

external_api_content.statement: Content from external APIs must be sanitized with strict config. Treat as untrusted. External sources may be compromised or malicious. Never trust external HTML without sanitization

safe_html_component.statement: Create reusable SafeHtml component that encapsulates DOMPurify.sanitize with dangerouslySetInnerHTML. Props: html (string), config (optional). Prevents developers from using dangerouslySetInnerHTML directly
safe_html_component.example: function SafeHtml({ html, config }) { const clean = DOMPurify.sanitize(html, config); return <div dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: clean }} />; }

use_safe_html_hook.statement: Create useSafeHtml hook that memoizes DOMPurify.sanitize with useMemo. Only re-sanitizes when HTML changes. Improves performance. Example: const clean = useSafeHtml(html, config);

security_package.statement: Centralize DOMPurify configuration in @company/security private NPM package. Export HtmlSanitizer class with predefined levels (strict, moderate, lenient). Ensures consistency across all applications. Single source of truth for sanitization rules

sanitizer_class.statement: HtmlSanitizer class provides static methods: sanitize(html, config), sanitizeComment(html), sanitizeRichText(html), sanitizeTrustedHtml(html). Each method uses predefined config for specific use case. Exported from @company/security

memoization.statement: Memoize DOMPurify.sanitize in React components with useMemo. Sanitization is computationally expensive. Only re-sanitize when HTML changes: useMemo(() => DOMPurify.sanitize(html), [html])

batch_sanitization.statement: When sanitizing arrays of content (lists of comments), sanitize all items in single pass. Reduces overhead. Example: items.map(item => DOMPurify.sanitize(item))

xss_tests.statement: Test sanitization removes common XSS vectors: <script> tags, event handlers (onerror, onclick), javascript: URLs, data: URLs with scripts. Assert malicious content removed, safe content preserved

config_tests.statement: Test sanitization configs: strict removes images and divs, moderate allows headings and lists, lenient allows tables and images. Assert each level's behavior is correct

always_sanitize.statement: ALWAYS sanitize HTML before using dangerouslySetInnerHTML. Even for content from database (defense in depth). Never trust any source of HTML. This is non-negotiable for security

strict_for_users.statement: Use strict sanitization for user-generated content. Users should only format text (bold, italic, links). No images, no custom styling, no data attributes. Prevents user-to-user XSS

defense_in_depth.statement: Sanitize at multiple layers: on input (before storage), on output (before render). If sanitization rules change, old data still safe. Also use CSP headers as additional protection

audit_config.statement: Document why specific tags/attributes are allowed. Review sanitization config regularly. As application evolves, sanitization rules may need updating. Comment each ALLOWED_TAGS and ALLOWED_ATTR decision

ssr_incompatibility.statement: DOMPurify requires DOM API, doesn't work in Node.js SSR by default. For SSR, use isomorphic-dompurify package which works in both browser and Node.js environments

over_sanitizing.statement:
Avoid over-sanitizing rich text content. If config too strict (only
allowed), users lose formatting ability. Balance security with usability. Use appropriate sanitization level for use case

sanitize_storage_vs_render.statement: Consider sanitizing before storage (database) AND before render. If only sanitize at render, changing sanitization rules doesn't protect old data. Sanitizing at storage provides defense in depth
